_meta.description: English seed data: 100 memory entries in 8 sections for manual testing and database population

items: My name is John Chen, I'm a full-stack engineer
I'm currently building a startup in the AI memory engine space
I primarily write code in Rust and TypeScript
I'm deeply interested in database internals, especially storage engines
Recently researching approximate nearest neighbor search algorithms like HNSW
I think Tantivy's BM25 implementation is quite impressive
Linear is a great project management tool for keeping track of tasks
I'm used to developing in Linux environments
I use both VS Code and Neovim as my editors
I enjoy functional programming style but I'm open to OOP as well
Compared to Python, I prefer Rust's type safety
For databases I prefer PostgreSQL, followed by SQLite
Front-end framework preference: React + Next.js, dislike Angular
I prefer clean API design and dislike over-abstraction
When writing tests I prefer Rust's native #[test] over mock frameworks
For text formatting I prefer Markdown over rich text
Deployment preference: containerized with Docker Compose for dev environments
Code style: I prefer 4-space indentation
Documentation and comments in English, code identifiers in English
Prefer async/await programming model over callbacks
I like imperative commit message style (conventional commits)
Color theme preference: dark mode
Prefer small functions, each function no more than 30 lines
Skeptical of ORMs, prefer writing raw SQL
API versioning: prefer URL path versioning over header-based versioning
Decided to use Rust as the primary backend language due to its performance and type safety
Chose redb as the storage engine because it is pure Rust with a clean API
Selected Tantivy for full-text search because of its BM25 and tokenization capabilities
Chose DashScope as the default embedding model provider
Decided to use a deterministic extractor as the fallback path, independent of external APIs
In-memory graph structure uses adjacency list representation for O(1) outgoing edge queries
Hash table uses open addressing instead of chaining to reduce heap allocations
Decided to use SimHash for semantic signatures — better for short text than MinHash
Vector index uses FlatIndex (brute-force search), more stable than HNSW at small scale
Serialization uses bincode instead of JSON or protobuf for optimal performance and size
Designed 10 recall channels for comprehensive multi-channel coverage
Decided to implement inverted index using redb's Table partitioning
Maximum spreading activation hops set to 2, balancing precision and latency
Designed write_batch API to support batch writes efficiently
Consolidation cycle set to 1 hour to avoid peak-time resource contention
Max seeds per channel set to 15 to ensure recall coverage
Using energy-based reranking instead of score-based — more aligned with bio-inspired design
Decided against introducing a Python dependency layer, keeping it all Rust for cohesion
Embedding dimension set to 1024 to match DashScope text-embedding-v4
Chose the Apache 2.0 open source license
The project uses a Cargo workspace with multiple sub-crates
Core crate: hippmem-core defines the data model and configuration
hippmem-store handles persistence, based on the redb embedded KV database
hippmem-model encapsulates external API calls and deterministic fallback implementations
hippmem-write is the write pipeline, converting raw text into indexed memory
hippmem-retrieval implements multi-channel seed recall and spreading activation
hippmem-consolidation handles memory consolidation: decay, compression, and Hebbian learning
hippmem-engine is the unified orchestration layer exposing 7 public APIs
hippmem-eval is the evaluation framework with 70+ test cases and 56 fixtures
hippmem-grpc provides a gRPC service interface for remote invocation
Full-text index based on Tantivy with BM25 scoring and multi-language tokenization support
Each MemoryUnit contains four parts: content, understanding, context, and semantic
AssociationLink represents edges between memories, supporting 8 edge types
Each memory builds an inverted index through AssociationKeys to accelerate recall
The spreading activation algorithm simulates biological neural activation
Multi-channel recall includes Entity, Topic, Temporal, BM25, Semantic, Binary and more — 10 total
The reranking phase combines energy scores with question-type-aware boosting
Supports dump (full JSONL export), list (paginated browsing), and traverse (graph exploration)
AlgoParams can be adjusted at runtime — channel coefficients, thresholds, etc. are hot-swappable
Test coverage approximately 85%, including unit tests, integration tests, and E2E evaluation
Completed CI/CD pipeline setup for the Rust monorepo
Fixed a deadlock bug in redb concurrent write scenarios
BM25 channel recall improved from 35% to 72%
Reduced embedding API call latency from 200ms to 50ms using batch mode
Resolved the deterministic extractor's handling of English acronyms
Completed Hebbian learning unit tests for the Consolidation module
Optimized inverted index key generation strategy, reducing collisions by 30%
Deployed v0.7 to staging environment, fixed 3 P0 issues
Completed the API reference documentation
Refactored write_internal, merging shared logic between write and write_batch
Diagnosed and fixed energy accumulation overflow bug in spreading activation
Changed seed_per_channel from a hardcoded constant to a configurable parameter
Implemented question-type detection for question-aware boosting
Fixed Tantivy index reader not refreshing, causing BM25 to return empty results
Completed JSONL formatted output for the dump API
Previously thought Tantivy's BM25 had issues, later found it was a reader reload problem — fixed and working
Originally planned to use JSON serialization, switched to bincode after benchmarking — 40% size reduction
Previously believed redb didn't support concurrent writes, turned out to be transaction isolation level misunderstanding — redb supports multi-reader single-writer
Earlier planned to introduce a Python wrapper, later decided against extra language dependencies — staying pure Rust
Previously used embedding dimension 1536 (OpenAI default), switched to 1024 after migrating to DashScope
The Rust community released Rust 2024 Edition, introducing several syntax improvements
DashScope text-embedding-v4 model achieved an MTEB score of 0.67
Tantivy 0.22 optimized tokenization performance by 40% throughput
redb 2.0 introduced Write-Ahead Log support, improving write performance
mem0 is another memory engine project, but its deterministic hashing makes its semantic channel weaker than HippMem
OpenAI's text-embedding-3-small supports dynamic dimension adjustment from 256 to 1536
Qdrant and Milvus are mainstream vector databases, but neither supports graph-based associative memory
Anthropic's Claude supports 200K context windows, but long-term memory still requires external storage
The MCP protocol enables AI models to access external tools and data through standardized interfaces
Rust's async/await ecosystem is increasingly mature, with tokio as the dominant runtime
I work as a core contributor on an open-source memory engine project
GitHub: johnchen, email: johnchen@example.com
10 years of backend development experience, 5 years with Rust
Previously worked on database kernel development at a major tech company, started my own venture in 2024
Based in San Francisco, works remotely, prefers async communication
